description.value:
Dverg who work stone professionally do not think of it as inert. They are not sentimental about this — the stone does not have feelings — but it has structure, and structure has implications, and those implications are readable if you know the grammar. A load-bearing arch will tell you if it is tired. A gas pocket will announce itself three steps before you reach it. Water reads differently in limestone than in granite. The knowledge is entirely practical and saves lives on a regular basis.

This project is a systematic training program in the Dverg art of geological reading: identifying load-bearing versus decorative stonework by visual and percussive analysis, recognizing the gas-seep signatures that precede dangerous underground atmospheres, locating water sources by moisture patterns and mineral staining, distinguishing stable from unstable ceiling rock, and estimating the age and construction quality of underground architecture. The training requires access to actual underground environments across varying geological types.

Rules Note: On completion, the hero gains edge on Reason tests to assess underground hazards: structural stability, atmospheric safety, water presence, and construction age/quality. Questions that a non-specialist would require a test for can often be answered by the hero with a brief observation (Director discretion). The hero automatically senses when a ceiling or gallery is at risk of imminent collapse — they feel the minute vibrations and see the dust-tell before anything audible happens.

system.prerequisites: A Dverg elder mason as instructor, access to varied underground environments (at least one mine, one natural cave system, one Dverg-constructed gallery), and time for repeated observation exercises.
system.projectSource: Dverg elder mason tradition — passed through the mason's guilds as a formal curriculum. Non-Dverg who learn it are rare enough that they are remembered specifically by the guilds that trained them.

yield.display: Edge on Reason tests for underground hazard assessment; auto-sense imminent collapse; many hazard questions answered by observation alone.
